title: Hooked rug monogram
subtitle: A single initial letter in hooked wool strips on monks cloth, approximately 25 × 25 cm, framed or finished as a decorative panel
excerpt: A hooked rug monogram is a small, focused project that teaches the basic hook technique and the colour balance decisions needed for a legible design: the letter in one colour, the background in another, with clear contrast between them. This entry uses narrow-cut wool strips hooked into monks cloth stretched on a frame.
sourceNotes: Hooked rug monogram design synthesised from general rug hooking practice. Monks cloth rug hooking with wool strips is the standard approach for fine-cut hooked rugs in the British and North American tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A evenly woven cotton fabric with a clear, consistent weave structure used as the foundation for hooked rugs. The regular weave makes it easy to pull loops through at consistent intervals.
A wooden-handled tool with a fine hook at the tip, used to pull folded wool strips up through the monks cloth backing from below, forming a loop on the surface.
Hooked loops that are cut open after hooking to produce a dense, velvet-like surface. Cut pile gives a softer, more coloured-looking surface than uncut loops.
The height of each pulled-up wool strip loop above the monks cloth backing, typically 6-10 mm for fine-cut hooking. Consistent loop height produces an even, flat surface.

content.text: A monogram hooked rug panel is a complete, legible design small enough to finish in a few sessions. The key to a clear monogram is contrast: the letter and the background must be far enough apart in value (light versus dark) that the initial reads immediately. Work the letter first, then fill the background around it. All loops are pulled up through the
of 6-8 mm for fine-cut hooking. The finished panel can be left as uncut loops or
for a denser, more velvety surface.

items.name: Wool fabric or yarn strips, letter colour
Wool fabric or yarn strips, background colour
Rug frame or embroidery hoop, 30 cm
Permanent marker for drawing the design

content.text: Draw or trace the chosen letter onto paper at the correct scale: the letter should fill approximately 60-70% of the 25 × 25 cm design area. Transfer the letter outline onto the monks cloth using a permanent marker, drawing directly onto the fabric surface. Keep the letter bold and simple: fine serifs and thin strokes are difficult to hook cleanly at 6 mm strip width. A bold sans-serif or block letter works best. Leave 5 cm of monks cloth around the design area for mounting the frame.

Cut or strip wool fabric into narrow strips approximately 6 mm wide. Recycled wool clothing (old skirts, jackets, blankets) provides a good range of colours and textures. Strips from woven fabric should be cut along the grain to prevent excessive fraying. Machine-cut strips are more even than hand-torn strips. Cut enough strips of each colour to fill the letter area and background completely before beginning to hook.

Stretch the monks cloth over the frame and secure with T-pins. Hold the wool strip beneath the backing with the non-dominant hand. Push the rug hook through a hole in the monks cloth from above, catch the strip, and pull up a loop to the correct height. Move one or two threads across the weave and pull up the next loop. Work around the outline of the letter first, then fill in the interior, keeping loops at a consistent height and in rows that follow the direction of the letterform.
Step 4: Fill the background and finish

Hook the background colour in straight horizontal rows from one edge of the design area to the other, working around the letter. Keep the background rows parallel and the loop height consistent with the letter loops. When all areas are hooked, trim any uneven loops on the back with scissors and press the back lightly with a damp cloth to even the surface. Mount in a frame or finish the edges by turning the monks cloth margin to the back and slip-stitching.

items.problem: The hooked loops pull back through the monks cloth when adjacent loops are added
items.cause: The loops are too widely spaced and there is not enough friction holding them in the weave
items.fix: Hook loops closer together: no more than 2 weave threads apart. The adjacent loops should press against each other firmly enough to grip the backing without pulling out.

items.problem: The letter edge is ragged and does not read clearly
items.cause: The hooking direction changed at the boundary between letter and background
items.fix: Hook the last row of the letter and the first row of the background in the same direction (both horizontal, or both following the edge). A consistent direction at the boundary creates a cleaner, more defined edge.

items.problem: The monks cloth buckles and does not sit flat
items.cause: The fabric was not stretched firmly enough on the frame
items.fix: Re-stretch and re-pin the monks cloth, pulling it firmly in all four directions before pinning. The cloth must be under sufficient tension that the weave structure is clearly visible and even across the design area.
